Introduction
Welcome to Module Two.
!
As we go through this module we will drill down deeper and deeper and walk you
through exactly how to do this, but in these two modules you are going to get the core
strategies and the tactics and the tools.
!
Life is never going to be the same.
!
In Module One we covered a lot of ground. We covered the core strategies and the
tactics.
!
In Module Two, we’re going to go deeper into sequencing and we’re going to start
molding your launch story. This is going to set you apart in the market.
!
In Module Two we’re going to cover the mental triggers. This is powerful stuff in every
area of your life, whether you’re trying to sell a product or in any interactions you have
with people. Asking for a date, or getting your kids to take out the trash, it doesn’t
matter. Mental triggers work everywhere.
!
This video I’m actually recycling from the last version of Product Launch Formula. It’s
because I did a great job and these mental triggers are timeless. It’s a great video, so I
decided to reuse it. I just don’t think I could make it any better.
!
Then we’re going to go into story. Story is incredibly powerful. I’m not going to claim
that I was the first person to bring story into marketing, because I certainly wasn’t.
!
Let’s just say I’ve really brought it into prominence within Product Launch Formula.
!
Story pulls people in. It’s how you attract people and hold people. You could hold them
for minutes to hours to days to weeks to years if you can tell story within your
marketing and use the elements of story.
!
I know there’s a lot of people that love story and it sounds awesome. If you just think,
“What the hell does story have to do with any of this?” Wait until we get to it. This will
set your marketing apart.
!
Then after that we’re going to go into the Business Launch Formula.
!

Mental Triggers
!
We’re going to talk about the mental triggers.
!
This is really important stuff because the mental triggers are the corner stone of how
we conduct these launches, or the psychology that’s going on. There’s a lot of mental
triggers that are built into our brains. They don’t change.
!

-- 3 of 47 --

If you get these mental triggers right, you’ll be able to use them over time. It’s not like
these fade away. They’ve been with us for hundreds and thousands of years and they’ll
still be with us thousands of years from now. If you want to look at evolutionary
psychology you can start to figure out why some of these exist, but it gets pretty crazy.
!
Stories is one of the mental triggers that really is the foundation of Product Launch
Formula.
!
In essence, the sideways sales letter, telling the story over time with that sideways sales
letter, what you’re doing is you’re creating an overall story of your marketing that goes
throughout your entire launch. That’s really what pulls people in.
!
We’ve been trained for thousands of years to listen to stories. Nothing is more engaging
than a good story. It’s almost like warm milk and cookies. When you start telling a story,
people just relax and they sit back and take it in.
!
This is the way that knowledge was initially passed down is through stories. It’s still
very powerful for influencing people.
!
You want to make your launch event-based.
!
This is something some people struggle with. You will see with our quick launches there
is some story around them. I’ll share the story of, one of my quick launches. It was a
tax sale, or the romance sale. I’m going to show you those. Those were based around
some external event, a wedding in one case, a tax surprise in the other instance.
!
If you’re doing a launch for a new product, it can be an event just because you have a
new product.
!
People love events. They get pulled in by events. They feel like they’re part of
something bigger than themselves. That’s part of a feeling of significance that we have.
!
Going through an event together—what you can do there—when you can provide an
event for people to go through together, that’s the basis of ritual. Rituals are literally the
most powerful experiences that we have. Rituals are unbelievably powerful. When you
have an event based launch, you’re starting to tie into that ritual instinct.
!
Community is incredibly powerful. This is something to remember. We act like we think
the people in our community are supposed to act. Virtual communities are really strong.

-- 4 of 47 --

You can literally create a virtual community and create the rules of engagement, how
people in that community are supposed to act.
!
If you can do this in your marketing, actually create a community and then create this
expectation in the community that people buy your product. It can be, not quite that
simple, but that type of thing can be really powerful in your launch. We’ll get to some
example of this.
!
Social Proof. Social proof is the idea that we all look to other people for clues on how
we should act.
!
This is another classic example I like to give. You’re driving into a town. It is dinner time
and you’re hungry. You’ve never been in the town before, maybe you forgot your
iPhone, so you can’t look up the restaurant. You have no idea which are the good
restaurants and which are the bad restaurants.
!
There’s a restaurant on the right side of the street that has six or seven cars in front of
it and there’s a restaurant on the left side of the street that’s got one car in front of it.
Which one are you going to go to?
!
Most people are going to go to the one with the six or seven cars in front of it because
those people must know something. Right? That’s basically what social proof is. We look
for clues on how to act by looking for how other people are acting.
!
If you can show your prospects that they are not alone, that other people are taking
action and buying your product and having success, then you’re way ahead. It’s really
powerful.
!
Scarcity is when there is less of something, people are inherently going to want it
more.
!
They will want something just by virtue of there being less of them. If there’s some
fancy Beanie Baby, but it’s hard to get. We see this all the time at Christmas time where
there will be the ‘it’ toy, the toy to have, and then all of a sudden there’s not that many
of them. People will just go crazy for them. They will go to crazy lengths to try to get
that toy.
!
Scarcity is incredibly powerful. Event-based launched naturally lend themselves to
scarcity. There’s an event that comes and goes and once it goes it’s gone, so there’s a
scarcity. There’s an inherent scarcity involved.
Copyright © All Rights Reserved Internet Alchemy, Inc. 2014 -2015 	Page ! 5

-- 5 of 47 --

!
Social proof and scarcity work really well together. When there is social proof it proves
that there’s demand. Inherently, that feeds into scarcity, and when there’s scarcity there
is inherently social proof because if something is scarce then a lot of people must be
going after that thing.
!
Authority. People will follow others in authority. This is why doctors wear a white coat.
It’s because as soon as they walk in the room we respect that white coat. You can
establish huge authority in your launch, just by giving away good prelaunch content.
!
People are going to assume that you are an authority and that will give you more power
in your communication with them.
!
Anticipation. There are few things that capture our attention and our imagination
more than anticipation does. We all look forward to events. We all look forward to
birthdays, holidays, and sporting events. This really pulls us in.
!
In the U.S. we have the Super Bowl. The Super Bowl is the only football game all year
long where there is a two week build up to it. There’s two weeks between the
conference championships and the Super Bowl. One of the big reasons for that is to
build the anticipation.
!
This is how people get pulled into your launch, by anticipating what’s coming next and
looking forward to what comes next.
!
Common enemy. It’s amazing what people have done when there is a common
enemy. They march off to wars and they stand in a line of bullets getting shot at them
because of a common enemy.
!
Often in marketing we’ll sort of fabricate a common enemy, whether it’s the IRS or
lawyers or some organization in your niche, or whatever. You can often build up a
common enemy and that makes the sale a lot easier. People will bond with you through
that common enemy.
!
Proof, we love to have proof in our launches. If we can show how people have brought
our product in the past and had success and we can show people that proof, that is
awesome.
!

-- 6 of 47 --

Controversy. That grabs people’s attention. It’s like watching a car wreck. People just
love to watch controversy. You can often talk about controversy in your launch and that
will grab people’s attention.
!
Commitment and consistency is that, basically people want to act consistently with how
they’ve acted in the past. There are all kinds of ways we can develop this commitment
and consistency.
!
Interaction and conversation. People would rather talk than listen. They’ll pay
attention to a conversation a lot longer than they’ll pay attention to a lecture. You can
make your launch feel like a conversation. It’s easy to do that with blogs where you’re
getting comments back and they’re interacting with each other. That grabs people’s
attention.
!
A lot of Product Launch Formula is about grabbing people’s attention and holding their
attention so that you can deliver a sales message to them.
!
Reciprocity is basically, if you give something to someone they’re going to want to
give something back to you. During a launch we’re generally giving great material,
great content, great prelaunch material away. This naturally builds up the reciprocity
trigger.
!
You’ve been giving to them, giving to them, giving to them. They’re going to be more
inclined to give back to you. Often what they’re going to give back in an order. They’re
going to buy from you.
!
Surprise. You don’t want to be predictable. The more you can be building some
surprises and unexpected turns in your launch, the better. It’s just novelty and that
captures people’s attention and keep them from being bored.
!
Likeability. People buy from people they know, like and trust. The more likeable you
are throughout your launch and your prelaunch materials, the better off.
!
Credibility. During a launch, you can publish material that establishes massive
credibility. This is very similar to authority. So many times people are disbelieving. They
get so many sales messages and sales pitches each day that they have their B.S.
detector at maximum most of the time, they just don’t believe people.
!
The beautiful thing is that during a prelaunch you have the time to basically prove your
credibility by giving away great content.

-- 7 of 47 --

!
Being interesting. This is pretty similar to the whole surprise and being novel. You
want to be seen doing unusual things. You want to be seen as being different. This is all
easier than you would expect. You don’t have to be very different at all, just a little bit
different.
!
I’ll tell you just by virtue of fact that you want to start or build your online business,
that you’re studying Product Launch Formula makes you different.
!
It doesn’t take much to be seen as being different. So many people have very boring
lives, just do something a little bit different and all of a sudden you’re seen as being
very interesting.
!
If you publish online, you basically automatically become a celebrity. The reason why?
!
The word ‘because’ is a very powerful word. “This is why we’re doing this, because of
this.” If you can give a reason why throughout your launch and throughout your sales
copy and every piece of your prelaunch content is really in effect a piece of sales copy,
it can be really powerful.
!
Tell people why you’re doing things.
!
The reason does not have to be great. The classic example from the book Influence by
Robert Cialdini.
!
They did a social science test where people were waiting in line to make copies at a
copier and there was a line at the copier and they had people walk up and ask if they
could take cuts at the front of the line.
!
They measured how effective that was.
!
I can’t remember what the numbers were, and the actual numbers are completely
irrelevant. Maybe 50% of the time they were successful. Then, they had them go up
and they made the same request asking, “Can I cut in front of you?” at the front of the
line. They would say, “Can I cut in front of you, because I’m late for class and I really
need to make these copies for my professor?”
!
As soon as they put that ‘because’ in there and gave a reason, all of a sudden the
compliance, their effective shot way higher. Their numbers went from 50% to 90% or
95%. It was significantly a much higher percentage of success just by giving a reason.

-- 8 of 47 --

Then they actually did the same thing and tested with a reason, but it was a lame
reason. It was a lousy reason. They said, “Hey, do you mind if I cut in front of you
because I really need to make some copies?” There was no real reason there what so
ever.
!
Because they used that word ‘because’ and because they gave a reason, even though it
was a very weak reason, not a very good reason at all, the compliance was almost as
high as when they gave a good reason. The reality is you just need a reason why. You
don’t need a very good reason why.
!
Next up is competition. If you can build up a competition this is another thing that
grabs people’s attention. If you have people competing for something, it’s very
powerful.
!
The take away sale. This is where basically you sell from a very powerful position
saying, “You can take it or you can leave it. I’ll be fine either way.” When you do a take
away sale like that it’s naturally very attractive. The more you act like you don’t need
something or someone, the more they will pursue you.
!
Simplicity. The launch might seem complicated at first from the outside, but you want
to keep your messaging very simple. The simpler you can keep your messaging, the
more powerful it is.
!
Specificity or concreteness is very similar to simplicity.
!
The more exact you can be the better. For example, Ivory soap is 99.25% pure. It’s not
100% pure. It’s not even 99% pure. It’s 99.25% pure. That .25% being so specific
makes it more believable than if they said it was 100% pure or 99% pure.
!
If you’re going to help someone lose weight, it’s better to have them lose seven pounds
in six days than to have them to lose 10 pounds in a month. The more specific you can
be the better.
!
If someone took your system and made money with it, it’s better if they made
$9,032.17 than if they made $10,000. Basically, if people see something that looks like
you rounded off a number, then they just think you’re not being precise and maybe
you’re taking some liberties with the numbers. So the more specific and concrete you
can be the better.
!

Finally we have emotion. This isn’t necessarily an easy thing to do, but if you can get
someone’s emotions involved it makes it a lot easier to influence them.
!
It’s just the way it is. If someone gets really excited about what they’re doing it’s a lot
easier to influence them. The state of mind that they’re in when they are excited is just
easy to influence.
!
Frankly, this is tough one to do. I haven’t seen this pulled off too often with success,
but if you can pull it off it’s really powerful.
!
So that was just a quick run through of the mental triggers. Again, we’re going to be
walking you through launches and showing you exactly how these are done.
!

The Launch Story
!
I can remember getting this email like it was yesterday... It is just so vivid in my mind I
had never seen any email like this before. It was completely different.
!
This was a long time ago. In fact, it was so different that I printed it out. This was back
in the days of the dot matrix printer before I could afford a laser printer, or they didn’t
even have inkjet printers.
!
I printed it out and it must have been six, seven, eight pages long, this email.
!
The reason it was so different is it was a sales letter. It was a sales letter for a product
that was called “Turn Your Computer into a Money Machine”.
!
At that point I had no business. I had never even heard of this information marketing
business. This idea of selling your knowledge, creating information or knowledge
products and selling them. It was something I had never even heard of.
!
I got this advertisement for “Turn Your Computer into a Money Machine”. I know that
sounds really cheesy right now, but it’s what it was. I printed it out and I sat there and
I read thing over and over. It was advertising this course that cost $97, plus shipping
and handling, so it was $99.50.
!

-- 10 of 47 --

I had very, very little money at the time. The disposable income for my family for the
entire year, meaning the money we had after we paid for our food, rent and insurance,
was $400 a year.
!
To spend $99.50 on this seemed ludicrous, but it just gave me this vision for this bigger
life for starting a business. It gave me this vision for having some control of my life, for
being able to support my family.
!
At that point I was Mr. Mom. I was staying home, taking care of two young kids. My
daughter was just a baby then. My son was a toddler, he was just a few years old. My
wife off was supporting the family. All I wanted to do was help support the family.
!
I got this email advertising this product and I read it over and over and over again for a
week. I just kept on going back to it. It just seemed to make so much sense, but it
seemed like such a leap that I could actually sell my knowledge, that anyone would buy
anything from me. It just seemed like this unbelievable leap.
!
Then I made this decision that changed everything. I said, “Boy, you know what? If I
look around this publishing industry, I see people publishing and making money. I know
the Wall Street Journal has been published for 100 years and they’re still publishing.
They must be making money, so I know people make money through publishing. Maybe
I can.”
!
I wrote out a check and sent the check off to buy that product “Turn Your Computer
into a Money Machine”. I got that back and that taught me the basics of information
marketing. Because of that product, a few years later I was producing my own
products. Here we are 15, 16, 17 years later and I’ve now sold over $20 million of
products.
!
The reason that I tell you that story is not so much for the point of the story. I just
wanted to illustrate how a story can pull you in. Did that one get you interested at all?
!
Were you wondering what was coming next and why I was telling you the story?
!
Stories are incredibly powerful. They are unbelievably powerful. They will set you apart
from your competitors. They will set you apart from your market. The beautiful thing
about product launches is they give you the time and the space to tell a story. You want
to turn your marketing into a story.
!

-- 11 of 47 --

You want to always be delivering your content and your marketing as a story. What we
want to do is create the story that starts at the beginning of your launch and arches
through your entire launch and really grabs people’s attention. This is how you cut
through the fog of marketing and really grab people.
!
When you use stories, you pull people into your marketing. That’s why you want to use
stories. You want to grab their attention. You want to create anticipation. You want
them to be hanging on, wanting to know what’s coming next in the story. You want
them wondering. How does this thing turn out?
!
That’s what keeps people reading a book, or watching Hollywood movies that are two
hours long. How do you keep people engaged for two hours? By telling a story. Stories
are how lessons have been passed down forever, since the beginning of time. Before we
even had language, back then we had stories.
!
Those stories were how wisdom was passed from one generation to another generation
to another generation. That’s what makes them so powerful. We are bred, it’s written in
our DNA to pay attention and learn from stories. Once we start to hear a story, the
filters go off and we believe that story.
!
Stories are really sticky, meaning that people really remember them. People remember
stories a lot longer than they are going to remember any lecture or lesson. Think back
to a seminar, class or workshop that you’ve been to two, three, four or ten years ago.
Try to remember something from that seminar or class. I bet you, if you think about it,
the odds are you’re going to remember a story.
!
If you remember a lesson or any real content, it’s probably because it was shared as
part of a story. It was wrapped into a story. Stories are what we remember. The other
key thing about stories is that we believe stories. They drop our defenses. It’s like we’re
back in Kindergarten again and we’re just sitting there listening to the teacher.
We believe stories. Stories are how we learn. Obviously, you have to be careful. You
don’t want to abuse this. You want to be truthful in your marketing and you want to be
truthful in your stories.
!
The thing is people will believe your stories way more than if you just start telling the
benefits or the features of your product. “This has got six DVDs.” or “This drill can turn
at turn at 20,000 RPM’s.” They’re not going to remember that. They’re going to
remember your stories.
!

-- 12 of 47 --

I’m a bit of a geek about stories. I’ve studied them quite a bit. If you’re interested in
this topic, the place I think you should start with is Joseph Campbell and his Hero’s
Journey.
!
It’s an amazing body of work. It’s a body of work for his entire lifetime, and it really
boils down the essence of storytelling throughout time and throughout the world.
!
The important thing to remember is that just about any story, any story is about the
protagonist. It doesn’t matter if it’s a comedy, if it’s a tragedy, if it’s a romantic story it’s
all about a protagonist, the hero’s efforts to secure whatever they desire.
!
Generally they are fighting for this thing, usually at great cost. It’s something that they
desire. The protagonists generally have to flirt with disaster before they can achieve
their aims and get what they’re after and live happily ever after.
!
That’s the master core formula behind the story. You have got a hero, or an anti-hero.
!
You’ve got a protagonist who is fighting the odds, fighting disaster to try to get what
they are going after. It’s about their struggles to overcome whatever is on that path to
what they’re looking for.
!
What does all of this have to do with your marketing? What does it have to do with
your product launches and with your business? That’s where we are going next...
!
!
!
Launch Story Themes
!
Now I want to drill down into some of the common story themes that we use in our
product launches.
!
These themes have been proven over the years and they work really well. They keep
people engaged, they pull people into your market and they will draw them into your
launch and into your world. One great big thing about these themes is they really help
humanize our marketing.
!
When you use these themes, people will really start to identify with you. In fact, they
will even want to be you. One of the things that these stories do is they really put us in
the same plain as the people watching our launch, intellectually, emotionally, and
Copyright © All Rights Reserved Internet Alchemy, Inc. 2014 -2015 	Page ! 13
PLF_Module2v1.0
GROUPINSIDERS.COM EXCLUSIVE

-- 13 of 47 --

socially. The deal is that a lot of times if you’re out there, just having a business sets
you apart.
!
It sort of turns you into a guru or a star or some type of an unattainable person. You
don’t really want that. You want people to be able to connect with you. You want that
expert guru positioning. You want to be seen as special but you also want them to be
able to connect with you. These stories will really allow you to do that. You’ll see as we
go through these.
!
Think about the movie Star Wars for example. That movie is a classic hero’s journey –
Joseph Campbell’s hero’s journey. In fact, George Lucas, who created the movies, based
Star Wars on that hero’s journey. In any case, Luke Skywalker ends up being an
incredible hero in that movie, but he’s the “every man.” He starts off as just a boy and
then he grows into this hero so you can identify with him.
!
One thing that is important to remember as you go through these themes is that you
can mix and match these themes.
!
You can use different themes in different parts of your launch. You can use more than
one theme. You can have parts of your launch that don’t have a theme at all. However,
I’ll just say this, the more you put these themes to work both in your launch and in your
entire business, the better. They are extremely powerful. The more you base your story
or your launch off of them, the better you’re going to do.
!
One final thing, I am a huge fan of being truthful in my marketing. It just makes
everything a lot easier and more congruent. Authenticity really does win. People have
their “B.S. detector” and it’s a finely tuned mechanism. They can see right through
inauthenticity like you wouldn’t believe. I’m a big fan of using these stories truthfully.
!
The good news is that these themes exist everywhere in our lives and you’re not going
to have to look very hard to find a way to put these themes to work in your business.
It’s often just a matter of looking at our lives in the context of these themes and then
finding the ones that apply or don’t apply at any given time.
!
Let’s get started. I’m going to start with my absolute favorite. This is my go-to theme.
One that I probably use more than any of the others. It is the reluctant hero.
!
I’ve used this one in some of my biggest, most successful launches. It works really well.
I think one of the reasons I love this one is that it is so aligned with my personality and
my life. This Reluctant Hero story theme was the basis of my first two really big six
figure launches back when I was publishing about the stock market.
!
One of them was when I published a home study course for stock market traders. The

ended up creating Product Launch Formula, and started teaching about Product Launch

The reality is that I really am constantly torn between building my business and my

The next theme is “loss and redemption.” The reason I’ve got these stars up here is
these are my “double-starred” favorites. Loss and redemption – this is a really powerful
story. Maybe you had something great and for some reason you lost it. It’s even better
if someone took it away from you, and you turned around and came back with it bigger,
better, and more powerful.
!
There are a lot of Hollywood movies with this theme. A lot of times you see this in
business, especially when someone is teaching about business. Perhaps they had a
business then lost the business. They went bankrupt, and came back and started again.
Or, maybe you were an athlete, got injured and came back after your injury. Maybe you
were a guitar player, hurt your hand and had to recover. During that recovery, you
learned things about teaching guitar and you were able to teach other people.
!

Loss and redemption turns you into a very sympathetic character. People are going to
root for you because you once had something and you lost it, especially if you lost it in
some unjust or unfair fashion. They’re going to root for you to get that back. Loss and
redemption is very powerful. It’s often a little harder to tie into a product launch than
the reluctant hero.
!
Often it’s a smaller part of the launch. In other words, it’s a part of the bigger story that
we’ll work into loss and redemption. If you chance using this in your launch, it is an
absolutely great theme.
Closely related to loss and redemption is the underdog story. People love to root for
an underdog.
!
That’s really what’s behind the loss and redemption. This is usually not something that
you’re going to base an entire launch around. Often you can turn yourself into the
underdog. For example, let’s say you’re fighting something in the industry – like maybe
an industry association that’s trying to hold people down, the IRS or some type of a
common enemy.
!
The common enemy is an amazing thing. People go to war and they kill each other over
a common enemy. This actually ties into revenge and that’s something we’re going to
get to a little further. You can’t do any better in your marketing than to have someone
rooting for you. If you could turn yourself into the underdog, it’s really powerful.
!
The next one is discovery. This is one we use along with curation. These next two fit
really well with product launches. Discovery is basically your origin story. I shared

how you got to where you are. How did you come to create whatever product you
created? How did you come to launch that product?
!
I just told you a little bit about my origin story at PLF and how I came to do that. I told
it within the context of the reluctant hero. If I was telling that further and was really
going into discovery, I would talk about how I learned to do these launches. I have a
whole story about that. How did I learn? I didn’t learn them from anyone. I had to
make it up.
!
The reason I made up these launches is because I didn’t know anything about sales,
selling or marketing. I just invented a new way to do it. I was scared to ask for the
order because I was scared to sell. When I first started out, instead of asking people to
give me money and asking for the sale, I kept giving them more cool stuff so they
would love me.
!
I finally worked up the nerve to sell to them. At it turns out, that ended up being my
product launch formula. That ended up being prelaunch content delivering great stuff

ahead of time. That’s my 10second version of my discovery story. Discovery is an origin

There’s also the curation model. The curation theme is another origin story. It’s the

Next is revenge. This really isn’t something that I use very much. I don’t really like it
because I think that if you’re seeking revenge it does not make you terribly attractive.
!
The bottom line in our type of business, and any type of business, is that you want to
be naturally attractive. We want to attract people to us. We want to attract prospects
and clients. If you look at someone who’s going after revenge, it’s not a very attractive
way to be.
!
It’s easy to be very unattractive when you’re using revenge as a plot line. Revenge is an
extremely powerful emotion. It does make for a powerful story, but you have to be
careful. It’s usually used in marketing where there is a common enemy. The common
enemy can be an association, organization or a government agency. They’re big, nasty
competitors who really try to shut you out or keep you down, and keep you out of the
market.
!
What you want to do if you’re going to use one of these revenge plot lines, is you need
to make sure that your revenge aligns with your prospects and their self-interest. If
your prospect wants something really badly, you have to make sure that the revenge
you’re going after also gets them the thing they want. At the end of your revenge story,
you really want it to grow beyond revenge. You want to say you see a bigger picture
and move towards service.
!
You want to go away from just hurting someone or getting back at someone. Maybe
that’s your initial impetuous but as you go forward, now you’ve grown and you really
just want to serve people. Otherwise, revenge is a real problem. There’s this big, bad
organization. Maybe it’s an industry or an organization doing some nasty things to keep
people back.

If your revenge story can help your prospects get rid of all those bad things, then you

The PS Path
!
Now I want to talk about the PS Path. That’s the Problem Solution Path.
!
We’ve talked about the big picture strategy, and the tactics. Then we talked about the
mental triggers, and I talked about story and the importance of using story and how it
pulls people in.
!
Now we’re going to talk about creating real value in your prelaunch. It’s going to
overlay on top of the triggers and it’s going to overlay on top of the story.
!
When I first started learning copywriting, way back over a decade ago, one of the first
and most effective formulas that I learned was what they call the Problem, Agitate,
Solve, so the PAS.
!
If this is your sales cycle, you would define a problem. Then you would get them all
worked up about that problem. Then you would solve the problem for them. The
solution would be, “Buy my stuff and here’s this great solution.”
!
“You have a stain in your clothes? Oh my gosh. It looks horrible. You’re going to have to
throw your clothes out.” That’s a problem, agitating about it. “You just spent a fortune
on this new suit and now it’s worth nothing.” That’s the agitation. Then the solution
would be, “Buy this magic powder.” Or “Buy this magic lotion and it will take care of the
stain, so you’ll be all taken care of.”
!
That worked then, not so much now. I mean it still works, but in this new world we’re
in, the Problem Solution Path works a lot better.
!
I want to thank my friend, Lisa Sasevich, who really helped me. She has this thing
PSPS. It’s actually sort of along the same lines as the Product Launch Formula in the
way we roll through things and the way we create huge value.
!
We’re going from Problem, Agitate, Solve and we’re going to our sideways sales letter
and we have our three pieces of prelaunch content. A lot of times people say, “Okay,

Jeff. I love Product Launch Formula because we create so much value. We give people

It’s usually the more ‘save the world’ people that are like that. They just love this idea

!
It’s about giving great content in the fashion of telling a story throughout your

!
On the other hand, some people say, “Oh man, if I give away good content it’s like I’m

!
I’m going to solve both of those. You don’t want to just give great content and give

!
Remember, here’s your three pieces of prelaunch content (PLC):
! 1. 	We’re really focusing on the journey or the opportunity for them to make a
change.
2. 	We’re really focused on their transformation
3. 	We’re starting to shift into ownership experience.
4. 	Then at the end you have your sales video or it could also be a sales letter.
!
Those are the three big pieces of your prelaunch sequence.
!
On top of all of this, we’re throwing in the mental triggers starting off with authority,
reciprocity, and community.
!
Moving through we’re starting to work in massive social proof and scarcity towards the
end. Laying all that throughout there.
!
What you need to think about, in terms of delivering the value in the Problem Solution
Path, is what the big problem is that you’re solving when they buy your product.
!
Maybe you’ve got a product about learning to play the guitar and your specific product
is about how to play entire songs. So many people buy guitars and they can actually

With Product Launch Formula, what’s the problem?
!
The problem is you have a business, a product, a brand and you want to get it out
there into the world. You want to build a business, make an impact and make a lot of
money, but you know you can’t because you’re using whole marketing.
!
The problem is you need to launch, and the solution in the end is to buy Product
Launch Formula. I don’t just present, like if I was in the old school sales letter, it would
be “You need to launch” as my headline something along that lines. Then I would
agitate telling them why they really need to launch and why their business could be
dead if they don’t launch. Then I would sell them the product.
!
That’s not how we do it within Product Launch Formula. We have these three pieces of
prelaunch content and we tie those together with a story and we create this Problem
Solution Path. We have this big problem and we want to take them to the solution. I’m
going to stick with Product Launch Formula as an example.
!
This will work for just about anything, except for artists, and I’ve got another video for
artists coming up. The problem is people need to launch. The solution is buy Product
Launch Formula. It could be the problem is they’ve got a guitar and they can’t play
songs and the solution is “Buy my course Learn a New Song Every Single Day.” It could
be that “You’re overweight, buy my pills, my solution, my retreat called Lose 30 Pounds
in 30 Days.”
!
We’ve got this big problem and solution. Now what we need to do is break that down
so that in each one of these videos we’re basically defining a smaller problem and
solving that problem. Really what we do is we define a problem, we give the solution
and then we define that next problem.
!
Then that next problem is going to be in this video. This is problem number one,
solution number one then problem number two.
!

Basically we just define it here at the end of our first video and then in the second
video we reiterate what that problem is and we give them solution number two. Then
we define problem number three and we reiterate it at the beginning of this video as
problem number three and then we solve problem number three. Then what do we do?
We define problem number four and that of course is the big problem and then we
solve number four when we sell the product.
!
This is the simple formula. It might not look simple, but it is. Define a problem, solve a
problem. Define the next problem, and tell them you’re going to give it in the next
video. In the next video, you reiterate that problem, you give the solution and then you
give them the next problem. Then you just keep on rolling forward.
!
There are times where it’s not this cut and dry. Maybe you have a problem that carries
over a couple of videos. In the first video you also have to define the big problem.
If we look at what I did in Product Launch Formula, in my last launch, what I did was I
said, “The initial problem is you need to launch. You absolutely have to launch. Every
strong brand, every strong product, every strong business starts off with a strong
launch.” That’s how I started it. I’m defining the big problem right off the bat.
!
Then what I did is I gave them a solution. The solution was the sideways sales letter,
and that’s the defining element of the Product Launch Formula. We started off with the
big problem. The solution is “You need to use the sideways sales letter.” Then a taught
the sideways sales letter in the first video. I define the problem and I gave a solution.
What we’re doing here is we’re building momentum, building trust and delivering value.
“Here’s this problem. Let me give you the solution.” That builds a lot of trust. Towards
the end of it, “I just defined the sideways sales letter, but now I’ve got to show you how
it actually works in practice.”
!
Then, “In this one I’m going to show you exactly how to use this in what we call the
Seed Launch, which is when you have no list and no product. I’m also going to tell you
about a couple of different launches. The idea is you need to use the right launch.”
That was the new problem. “You now understand the sideways sales letter and the
concept of the launch, but let me show you how it actually fits within three styles of
launches for three different types of businesses.”
!
Then I brought them into this one, at the beginning I reiterated that problem. I said,
“You’ve got to know what type of launch to use.” Then I actually taught the seed launch
along with the other two styles of launches. I gave them another problem at the end of

video number one. I reiterated the problem at the beginning of video number two, then
solved the problem in video two by teaching it and defined another problem.
The problem is, “Now I have to show you the full blue print of how everything all ties
together here.” Then I took them into the third one and I gave them the entire
blueprint of the whole Product Launch Formula.
!
A lot of times the problem is you need to do something. The solution is, “I’m going to
teach you how to do it. The next bigger problem is now you might want to go deeper,
and the solution is to buy the product.
!
If you look at what I did in my third video was the Product Launch blueprint. I sort of
taught the whole thing. Product Launch Formula is one big mind map. Within there, at
the end of the video I say, “Now I know a lot of you want to take this deeper. You want
to learn more about this and learn exactly how to do it and go deeper in your training.”
The solution then in the sales video was to buy my product.
!
That’s the Problem Solution Path. It doesn’t have to be this exact where you’re doing a
different problem and solution in every single video. It works really well if you can. You
want to have this hook for each video that there is a reason to watch the video because
there’s this big problem.
!
You could take one problem and define it over two or three videos. The thing you don’t
want to do is that good old fashioned Problem, Agitate, Solve where it just feels like one
big sales letter. Within here you need to be solving smaller problems.
!
That’s the whole thing. It’s about taking that really big problem and breaking it down
into pieces and giving solutions to those pieces along the way while you’re still working
into this final solution, and the solution is to buy your product.
!
That is the Problem Solution Path. That’s how you really deliver massive value without
giving away the farm, and deliver massive value in a way so it leads into the sale.
!
!
!

!
Here’s part two. The advanced part of the PS Path.
!
There are a couple of subtleties here. Remember that old Problem, Agitate, Solve? A lot
of people don’t like the agitate part. They don’t like that ‘stick of the knife in the wound’

type of thing because we want to save the world, provide value and self. You still need

When you define the problem, you do have to agitate. You do have to dimensionalize.

The Business Launch Formula, Part 1
!
Now I want to focus on the Business Launch Formula.
!
Imagine doing a really great launch of your business. What would that mean for your
business? What would it mean for your life if you did a great launch? What would that
mean in terms of money? How much money would that be for you? What would that
mean in terms of your list build, like how many people you added to your list?
!
What would it mean in terms of your positioning in the marketplace, where you would
be seen as an expert? What would it mean for your lifestyle? Could you go full time, get
that new home, get that new car, take that vacation, or send your kids to a great
school?
!
It’s pretty cool to think about what a really great launch could do. People have all
different kinds of numbers when they think about this. Some people think, “I’m going to
do $10 million on my launch.” Some people say, “It would be really awesome if I did
$300 in my launch.” It’s all over the place.
!
For me, I had very humble beginnings. I didn’t have a lot of outside reference points to
look at. I didn’t have any entrepreneurs in my family. I wasn’t thinking that big. I was

relationship with the list.
!
You often will actually build your list just through word of mouth even if you don’t have

Now you’ve built your relationship to your list, and then you come back and do another

You do that launch for your next product, and you‘re building your relationship and

In the last video I walked through some of the results that John has had in his

you’re going to do it as well.
!
Let’s just say this is your list. One of the fundamental things that you have to
understand is that you will end up with a bunch of different lists. There are a couple
primary types of lists. There’s the prospect list, the people who haven’t bought from you
yet. Then there’s the buyers list, and that’s the people that have bought from you. In
our business we call it an owners list.
!
This is the prospect list. I’m going to write it really small. When John started out he had
those 1800 people on his prospect list. This is a newsletter. Every month he published a
newsletter. This will go basically forever until someone unsubscribes. This is your main
list, for John it was his newsletter list. What he did was within this list he ran a little
launch sequence for the game.
!
Maybe it’s launch day for the game, and now if someone buys from him, then they join
his owners or buyers list. Basically when someone buys from you they go on your
buyers list. Let’s say this is the launch of his board game and now these people went
into his buyers list.
!
Now, his next launch is for the herbal kit, so he would there is—this is what we call a
launch list. He would basically create a squeeze page. He would send people to the
squeeze page. He might send out an email that says, “If you’re interested in being on
the list for the herbal kit.”
!
That’s typically not what you’ll say, but you’ll talk about whatever the opportunity is for
the herbal kit, send people to a squeeze page and now he’s got them on his launch list.
In the launch list, maybe there’s two, three or four emails, then there’s a chance for
them to buy right here.
!
This is where they bought for the board game. This is where they buy for the herbal kit.
Now what do you do? If they buy the herbal kit they go on to the buyers list for the
herbal kit. Now you’ve got a couple of different buyers lists.
!
Depending on what they’re buying and how your business lays there might be one
buyers list. All the different list systems manage lists and prospects in different ways,
but conceptually you’ve now got buyers.
!

I’m sorry I missed another key piece. Now you’ve got emails going out to the buyers

!
Now he’s coming up on his membership site, and he creates another launch list now for

!
He’s got people joining his launch list for his membership site. You’re going to mail this

One of the things about buyers lists is, in my experience someone on your buyers list is
worth 15 to 20 times what someone on your prospect list is worth. I would do a launch,
back in my stock market days I’d have 5,000 people on this prospect list. Then I’d have
500 people on my buyers list in the early days.
!
I would come up with a new product and do a promotion, and I would do more sales to
this 500 person list than that 5,000 person list. Once you sell something to someone the
first time, it’s much easier to sell something to them the second time.
!
John takes them through the sequence here for the membership site, send through a
sequence. Now they get a chance to buy right here. It ends up with a decision to buy. If
they buy, what happens? They go on his buyers list. Basically they’re part of the
membership site which is a buyers list.
!
The next time he might want to do a home study course and he creates another launch
list for that with a sequence for that. What’s he going to do? He’s going to peel people
off his main list, in other words give them the opportunity to join this list. He’ll have a
squeeze page. The squeeze page will offer some great prelaunch content.
!
They come from his prospect list. Each one of these buyers lists he’ll also mail each one
of them and send them in here. He’ll create another launch list.
!
What you’re doing here is—if you’re going to do another launch for this, an all-out
launch and a whole bunch of emails, maybe these people are used to getting emails a

couple of times a month and you’re going to keep up that publication schedule. You
don’t want to start hammering them with all these emails for your upcoming launch.
Generally, you’ll create that launch list, then you’ll encourage people to move over from
this list to this list. When I say encourage them that means you’re going to mail them
and send them to a squeeze page. The squeeze offers some cool things, some cool
videos, bonuses, whatever. That leads them into the sequence.
!
Generally with these launch lists, they’re sort of an episodic type thing. When someone
goes on my launch list, I’m emailing them through the launch sequence, after that
launch sequence is over I’m usually not mailing that list again because they’re still over
on this list.
!
The other thing is if you move them over to this launch list here and you send a whole
bunch of emails, and usually if you’re going to launch you do send a bunch of emails.
Maybe at some point they decide that they’re not interested. They can unsubscribe but
they’ll only be unsubscribing from this list.
!
If they want to unsubscribe from all of your lists they can do that as well, but generally
they’ll unsubscribe from this list but they’ll still be on your main list or on your buyers
list. This is how you can be, basically, doing a launch.
!
In most of my businesses I’ll do them three or four times a year. I know people that are
doing a lunch every single month. How do you do a launch to your prospect list every
month? If each one of these dots were a month, now all of a sudden you send them a
whole bunch of email here and a whole bunch of email here for each different launch
you might have some list fatigue.
!
Instead what you do is you give them the opportunity to join these launch lists and take
part in that launch and get the great prelaunch content that you’re going to put out.
This gives you the opportunity to continue to launch after launch after launch, not burn
out your buyers list and just keep on rolling along.
!
Then when you come up with another idea for another product you just create another
launch list and another sequence and you’re sending them over.
!
If you’re just start starting out—when John did his first launch he didn’t come out with a
separate launch list. He just did his full out launch to his full list. In general you typically
want to do a new launch list when you’re coming out with a new product, especially if
it’s somewhat of a departure from what you’ve done in the past.
!

They bought the board game. They’re getting a newsletter, but that board game might
be really different from the home study course. The home study course might be on
flower essences or something which is related and a great fit for his list, but slightly
different. Maybe not everyone here is interested in flower essences, so you put them
over on that list for people that are interested in that flower essence launch.
!
That is how you do it. That’s how you orchestrate this without burning out your list and
this is how you continue to build that list relationship. Every business will be different.
How you organize your lists will be different. It will evolve over time.
!
One final piece is, when you get people to go through this opt-in process, an opt-in for
that new list, that reinvigorates the lead. The lead being the person on the list. In other
words when someone first joins your list, they are most responsive when they first join
your list. Then over time on average they get less responsive. They see a bunch of your
emails and get less responsive over time.
!
That’s just a rule of lists that goes back 100 years. When you get them to step up and
opt in again, that rewarms them up and that really reinvigorates the list. It is generally
a great thing when you’re going into a launch for a new product to create that new
launch list.
!
That’s sort of the organization of lists within the Business Launch Formula. Again, there
are lots of different ways you can do this, but conceptually this is how it goes.
!
!
!
The Business Launch Formula Part 3
!
This is next example is actually my example, back when I started my first online
business.
!
This is where I was teaching people about the stock market. I had no credentials what
so ever. I wasn’t a stock broker, registered investment advisor or anything like that. I
was just someone who had a passion for the stock market and spent a lot of time
studying it.
!
I started a newsletter about the stock market. That first newsletter went out to about
19 people. I mentioned that to you earlier. It was actually 17 people. It was 19 email

addresses. One was my second email address and one was my wife’s email address, so

Here’s that list. This is just like John started, except he had that 1,800 people. I had 19

!
How do you go from 19 people to 80,000 people? Very simple. I really focused on

many people I got to opt in every day. I had a graph and I graphed every single day
how many people I got to opt in.
!
It started off with every few days I would get one. Then it got up to the point where I

35 people per day. That’s like 1,000 people a month. That’s 12,000 people a year. I did
that for seven or eight years. Do the math and you end up with around 80,000.
!
Of course you get some unsubscribes along the way, but every now and then I would
do some promotions and stuff. I ended up just over 80,000 people. All along I kept on
testing and tweaking the process, working on getting people to opt in.
!
In any case, this is this list. The thing about this list was (I’m going to do a little
modification right here) it wasn’t just a list it was a list of investors. I don’t know if they
were investors. They were people interested in investing. They were interested in the
stock market from an investing stand point.
!
For those of you who don’t know much about the stock market, there’s investors and
there’s also traders. Traders are interested in the market as well, but investors usually
have a longer time frame. Investors that are in and out of the market. Investors go in
the market and stay in the market.
!
There’s this other universe. It’s similar, both interested in the stock market, but these
folks are more intensely interested and more short-term, in and out of the market. I
had an interest in both of these areas. I had knowledge about both of these areas.
I started this list, and after I got that list, I think it was around 1,600 people after four,
five or six months.
!
At that point I actually had someone who was a mentor. That mentor was a trader, and

publishing.” I’ve got to mention, first of all these are free. This is a free newsletter. I

My mentor said to me, “Let’s partner up and create a product for traders.” That’s what

launch. This was that very first launch.
!
What I did was I created a launch list. I didn’t call it a launch list at the time. I didn’t
know what a launch list was, no one did frankly. I created a second list. I invited people
to join this list, gave them a bunch of free content for about a month, then I made this
off for this product.
!
We did this $1650 and we were off to the races and kept on building from there. We did
launch after launch after launch. That’s where I did the $6000, then we did the $8000
and we kept on getting better. Eventually we did a $17,000 launch, but we kept on
getting better, doing about two to three launches a year.
!
Basically, I tool this investing list. Then I rolled off a launch list and then I sold people
into this trading list. We kept on doing these occasional launches. We kept this for sale
every day of the year, but two to three times a year we put together a sale, a
promotion.
!
We would offer a discount. We would offer a bonus with an extra report, extra
subscription time and we would create a launch, and do a promotion for that and do
another launch.
!
Then I’m thought, “Okay, well this is going along pretty good.” Then I found the middle
ground. What I learned in publishing to both of these groups is that there was this
middle group. They were people that traded more often than the investors, but they
traded less often than the full-out traders. These are sort of long-term traders or short-
term investors so to speak.
!
I found out that this big group was out there. I found that out because these people
would always write back to me what they wanted. What we did was we created another
launch list. We created this product, created another launch list. Didn’t create a
product, actually created a launch for it, sent people from the investors list and sent
people from the traders list into this launch list.

It’s a little more complicated because actually this traders list, we had a prospect list

!
That was the first launch I did that did over six figures. That was a great launch,
beautiful launch, it was incredible. It was all profit. We had no partners. We had no
costs. We hadn’t created the product yet. The product was actually, they were going to
get a daily email from us telling them what we thought was going to happen the next
day in the stock market.
!
Literally there was no cost. We were going to send them for a year’s subscription one
email a day, so we were going to send them 250 short emails that said, “We think the
market is going up, or it’s going down.” Literally, no costs to fulfill. There’s no JVs,
nothing, just $106,000. After merchant expenses we ended up with $102,000 or
$103,000.
!
That was completely awesome. The thing is we just responded to the market demand.
These people said, “Hey, we know that you’re doing this short-term trading stuff, but
I’m not a short-term trader. I’m a day trader. How can I use all of your wisdom?”
!
These folks said, “Well, you know that day trading stuff is really cool, but I’ve got a job.
I can’t do that. I need something a little longer term. I’d like to trade on a daily basis.”
We just responded to that market demand, made up this offer, put people on a launch
list and ran them through the sequence. Now we’re starting to talk about some real
money. Right?
!
You know what? It didn’t end there, of course it doesn’t. Just like John Gallagher, you’re

did these paid webinars. We would sit there and watch the market, and make
comments throughout the day. People would pay us $200 to $300 to watch our
comments for a day a two, just to learn from us.
!
What did we do then? We created another launch list. We just fed people from each
one of these into this launch list and then we sold the webinar. It didn’t end there. I

more. We would do these a few times a year.
!
Then there was the home study course. What did we do? We did another one. We just
drove everyone into the home study course. We rolled that out. The first time we rolled

that out, that thing did $110,000. Again, that was no partners, no affiliates. We did

The Business Launch Part 4
!
I know you love examples, so I wanted to give you one more Business Launch Formula
example.
!
Look at those first two I gave you. They’re fundamentally different, but still there were
the common themes. Starting by building that list, continue to build that list, creating
products on what the market wants, creating a portfolio of products over time, not
getting it perfect the first time, starting small and growing from there.
!
I could give you dozens and dozens of examples and I've mentioned Susan Garrett. She
started off with that ebook, that $27,000 launch, which was great but she has grown
from there. She’s had one great product after another. We see this over and over.
!
It was in my Platinum group. The great thing about my Platinum group is I meet with
them three times a year, so I get to really dig inside their businesses. We see this
example over and over and over where people are just building a list, do a launch then
have their next product defined by the interaction they have with both their prospects
and their buyers lists. It works over and over.
!
There is almost never a big master plan to start with. It’s building the list, building the
relationship, listening to what people want. This next example is for my Product Launch
Formula business, where I teach people about online marketing. This is my biggest and
best business yet. I’m sure I’m probably going to have another one, but this one is
doing pretty well.
!

In 2003, I was still publishing about the stock market. I went to a marketing seminar. I
met a bunch of people, and thought, “This is really cool.” A lot of the people I met were
selling online marketing stuff. By that time I had been selling stuff for seven years, and
had built up a lot of experiences getting pretty good at marketing.
!
I knew I was pretty sure about marketing. I looked around and saw these people in
that business and thought, “This is a pretty cool business. I might want to be in that
business teaching online business. I might want to be in that business at some point.”
!
I built up some great relationships at that first seminar. I went home and I put together
a squeeze page and I started doing a little tiny list building, very, very humble. I think in
the next couple of years I got between 1,000 and 2,000 people on that list. It was
nothing impression, especially given the numbers I had in that stock market niche
where I was publishing to this huge list.
!
I had a significant business making a bunch of money. I just put up a squeeze page and
let traffic trickle into that squeeze page. I didn’t really do anything to drive traffic to it,
just built it through word of mouth.
!
I started to build relationships. Every now and then I would mail about an affiliate off,
but maybe twice a year maximum. That was my thinking, “I might want to be in that
business someday. I don’t know. I might want to be there some day. I’m just going to
start publishing. We’ll see what happens.”
!
I wrote an autoresponder sequence. In fact the site is still up. It’s at SixInSeven.com.
It’s ugly, ugly, ugly. You can see what my sites looked like back in 2003. Since I’m
putting this out there, maybe I’ll go and put a decent page up at SixInSeven.com.
That’s for six figures in seven days.
!
When I first went to that seminar, I started telling people about my launches. I had just
come off of doing that six figure launch. It seemed really cool in the day. It still is really
cool, but in this era where I’m doing these million dollar launches and my friends are
doing million dollar launches, it’s almost quaint. Back then it was really impressive.
!
I shortened that six figures in seven days to SixInSeven.com and I put up the squeeze
page and started building the list. I only mention that because this is more than two
and a half years before I would ever actually sell anything in that market. My first
thought was, “I might want to play in this market at some point. I’m going to create a
squeeze page.”
!

I wrote some autoresponder copy and that was it. I didn’t do anything to it what so
ever. Like I said, in two and a half years I maybe sent out three promotions total, I
think. Then an interesting thing happened in March, 2005. My partnership broke up.
!
You know that happens to partnerships, but this happened rather suddenly. One day I
had a business. I got a phone call, and picked up the phone. My partner didn’t want to
be in business anymore so that business went away.
!
That was in early 2005. I was scheduled to speak at Yanik’s Underground Online
seminar. Yanik is one of the friends I had made. Yanik Silver was one of the prominent
internet marketing trainers at the time. I had gotten to know him and become friends.
He had asked me to speak at an event and I said, “Sure, I’ll come out and speak.”
!
That was sort of my coming out party, to go out and speak at this marketing event. I
think it was at the end of March, 2005 when the event was going to be. I was planning
on just going, speaking, walking off the stage and not doing anything. Then when that
business blew up about three weeks before that I thought, “Huh, maybe I’ll do a
training about these product launches. Maybe I’ll put together a training and sell the
training from stage.”
!
That’s what I did. There were 200 or 300 people in the room. I got up there and for an
hour and 25 minutes I gave the best content that I had. I taught everything I knew
about product launches. At the end of it I said, “If you want to learn more about this,
you want to get some more training, you can get my course. I’m putting together a
training for this for $3000.” I had six people buy.
!
$18,000 at that point was great because my business had gone away earlier that
month, so it was great to bring in that money, but really selling to six people when
there are 200 to 300 people in the room was really lousy results. It was because I don’t
know how to sell. I wasn’t a sales person. I wasn’t a pitch man.
!
I took those six people at $3000 each. That was my initial seed launch for Product
Launch Formula. I called it the Product Launch Workshop. What I did was, I took those
six people and thought, “That’s not enough to teach, because I’m going to do a series
of calls.”
!
I had made a bunch of friends now, and I had a mastermind group and I invited them
all in on a comp basis. They didn’t have to pay. We added about 30 more people in on a
comp basis. We ended up with 36 people in this first training. Six paid, 30 for free, but

it gave me enough to do the training. There might have been more. There might have

We had this good training. I promised them five teleseminars. I delivered those

saying, “Okay, on that topic (maybe the topic was prelaunch content) what questions
do you have? What didn’t I cover? What do you need to know? What did I do really well
on that call? What do I need to do differently? How do I need to train you better?” I did
that after every call.
!
The feedback I got basically taught me how to teach product launches. I knew how to
do product launches. I had done a whole bunch of them. At that point in 2005 I had
done dozens of them, but I’d never taught it.
!
This is how I learned to teach. This is a key principle of the seed launch which we’re
going to be getting to in this module. That’s how I learned. This is the Product Launch
Workshop (PLW).
!
I was thinking, “This is working great. I worked with these people. They got some great
results. Since it was so interactive they loved it. I did a great job with it because it was
interactive. I got great word of mouth. I got some great case studies out of that.”
I had case studies, and I’m thinking, “I’m liking this business. I’m liking the potential
here, even though I only sold six.” I knew it because I was a lousy sales person. I
wasn’t experienced at selling from stage. What I did was I created version 1.0 of
Product Launch Formula (PLF).
!
I had that tiny little list I’d started building. I had about 2,000 people on that tiny little
list, but I also had all of these JV partners. The JV partners were all those friendships I
had made in the prior two years, all the relationships. Because I had this great word of
mouth coming out of these people, and because I had been building all of these
relationships, they were all interested in promoting.
!
I sent my 2,000 people into the prelaunch. My JVs all sent their people. All of a sudden
I think I came out of this with about between 15,000 and 20,000 people on my list that
I built through this launch, just by these JVs. That was just absolutely amazing.
This 1.0 launch did $600,000. I went on to do much bigger launches. Certainly launches
in this business are much bigger now, but that was one of the biggest launches ever at
that point.
